title: Soulshard

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Soulshard as a 2D side-scrolling platformer built around 30 discrete levels, trap avoidance, and using the environment to progress, with single-player and local co-op play. The copy does not establish meaningful combat, RPG systems, exploration structure, or action-platformer fighting, which points away from action_platformer and toward a non-combat precision/platform-navigation identity. Minimalist pixel-art presentation and a dark limbo-like setting are also directly supported by the store text.

overall_note: The rail is directionally solid: most candidates target side-view co-op-capable platforming with environmental hazards and low combat emphasis. The main spread issue is that several candidates lean more heavily into co-op puzzle design than Soulshard, which seems more like a solo-capable trap-navigation precision platformer with co-op support rather than a fully co-op-defined game.
anchor_summary: Soulshard appears to be a 2D level-based precision/puzzle-platformer centered on trap avoidance, environmental traversal, and optional local co-op, with little evidence of combat or exploration-heavy progression.
gap_notes: Several candidates are more explicitly puzzle-coop or partnership-dependent than the anchor, which may overstate Soulshard's co-op centrality.
The set does well on side-scrolling traversal and hazard navigation, but fewer picks land squarely on minimalist trap-heavy precision platforming.
No candidate is a clearly wrong genre match, though some are looser due to stronger puzzle/gimmick identity than the anchor.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on 2D stage-based traversal, hazard navigation, and local co-op platforming, though it is more puzzle-driven and more strictly co-op-dependent than Soulshard.
Strong fit on side-scrolling obstacle navigation, environmental interaction, solo or local co-op play, and low-combat challenge structure; more cinematic and less minimalist than the anchor.

Good match for precise platforming, trap-heavy stage challenges, and solo/couch co-op structure, but its stunt-puzzle/twitch identity is a bit more specialized than Soulshard's simpler trap-avoidance loop.
Reasonable comp through 2D puzzle-platforming, solo/local co-op support, and low combat emphasis, but it is more narrative-adventure oriented and less precision/trap-focused than Soulshard.
Understandable as a co-op obstacle-course platformer comparison, but its identity is more gimmick-led and heavily built around two-character cooperation than Soulshard appears to be.

title: Aquamarine: Explorer's Edition

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently portray Aquamarine as a single-player, turn-based sci-fi survival/exploration adventure focused on foraging, cultivation, salvage, loadout upgrades, navigational challenges, and environmental puzzle solving in an alien ocean. The strongest supported signals are cursor-driven/point-and-click interaction, authored linear mystery framing, non-combat play, and environmental puzzles. However, none of the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes cleanly fits this combination. It is not well-supported as hidden_object_puzzle because the evidence emphasizes survival, navigation, and environmental problem solving rather than hidden-object scenes. It is not well-supported as management_tycoon because there is no strong business/optimization/tycoon loop. It also does not cleanly map to exploration_survival_adventure because the available archetype implies a more standard real-time survival-adventure identity than the game's turn-based, comic-book, puzzle-forward structure. Because the evidence is solid about what the game is, but does not strongly support any allowed archetype, rejection is the safest taxonomy outcome.

overall_note: The set has a credible top comp and several understandable neighbors, but it leans broad and misses an especially tight same-identity match. Best candidates emphasize turn-based expedition survival or low-combat discovery; weaker ones drift toward real-time sandbox survival or narrative adventure without the same survival-puzzle loop.
anchor_summary: Aquamarine: Explorer's Edition is a single-player turn-based survival/exploration adventure with strong resource pressure, navigation, environmental puzzle solving, crafting/upgrades, and mystery discovery in an alien ocean. Its identity is defined more by deliberate turn-based survival-puzzle progression and low/no combat than by pure action, open-world sandboxing, or narrative-only adventure.
gap_notes: No candidate is a near-'same' match for Aquamarine's specific blend of turn-based survival, environmental puzzle solving, and authored mystery progression.
The rail over-indexes on shared exploration/setting and under-serves the game's puzzle-forward, low-combat structure.
A stronger rail would likely include more turn-based survival/adventure hybrids or puzzle-led expedition games rather than open-world real-time survival comps.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Best fit in the set: turn-based expedition structure, survival/resource management, procedural hazards, and roguelike tension align closely, even if it is more party/event driven and less environmental-puzzle focused.

Shares journey-based survival, supply management, hazard mitigation, and campaign progression, but its wagon-travel/event structure is less exploration-puzzle driven and less discovery-focused than Aquamarine.

Very strong overlap in alien-ocean survival, scavenging, crafting upgrades, wildlife study, and escape-home framing, but the real-time open-world sandbox loop is materially different from Aquamarine's turn-based, puzzle-forward design.

Good match on sci-fi mystery, low combat, deliberate exploration, and puzzle-like discovery, but it is primarily a narrative/language-deciphering adventure rather than a survival-management expedition game.

title: Resident Evil Requiem

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions repeatedly frame the game as 'survival horror' and add concrete loop details: combat, investigations, puzzles, and resource management. The corpus also explicitly says players can freely switch between first- and third-person views, supporting a modern single-player campaign survival-horror structure rather than a purely cinematic or purely psychological horror title. Story setup emphasizes investigating mysterious deaths in Raccoon City while escaping lethal threats, which supports detective_mystery plus survival_escape within an authored linear campaign.

overall_note: The rail has strong Resident Evil and survival-horror instincts at the top, but it misses an obvious must-keep benchmark and includes a mislabeled DLC entry that weakens confidence in the set.
anchor_summary: Resident Evil Requiem is positioned as a modern single-player survival-horror campaign built around tense combat, investigations, puzzle-gated progression, resource management, and a mix of first- and third-person play, with one side leaning classic RE survival horror and another leaning more action-horror via Leon.
gap_notes: Resident Evil 2 is the clearest benchmark here and should lead the rail.

Resident Evil Village, Resident Evil 4, and The Evil Within 2 are all plausible neighbors, but they are less exact than Resident Evil 2 because they skew more action-forward, more linear-set-piece-driven, or more semi-open/stealth-oriented.

The entry requested as Resident Evil 7: Biohazard is actually Resident Evil 7: Biohazard - End of Zoe, a DLC expansion with a more specialized hand-to-hand combat profile, so it is not a proper 'same' comp for the anchor.
Given the anchor's classic survival-horror plus investigation framing, the set feels light on exact puzzle-gated, resource-managed survival-horror comps beyond Resident Evil 2.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Near-direct gameplay match: modern RE survival horror with tense exploration, scarce resources, puzzle solving, dual campaigns, and Raccoon City structure.
Strong modern RE survival-horror fit with inventory pressure, pursuit, authored progression, and first-person combat, though it leans more action-forward than the anchor's investigative/classic side.
Good survival-horror neighbor with limited ammo, exploration, horror combat, and detective framing, but its semi-open hubs, stealth, and trap crafting make it looser.

Relevant especially for the Leon/action-horror side: over-the-shoulder combat, horror campaign, and some resource pressure, but it is more combat- and set-piece-driven than classic puzzle-gated survival horror.

candidate_reviews.candidate_title: Resident Evil 7: Biohazard - End of Zoe
candidate_reviews.rationale: This is not Resident Evil 7 proper but the End of Zoe DLC; its hand-to-hand, side-story combat focus is a poor match for the anchor's broader survival-horror campaign identity.

title: Laysara: Summit Kingdom

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Laysara: Summit Kingdom as a 'challenging city builder' and a 'pure city building experience' focused on economy, resource management, production chains, settlement expansion, transport/logistics optimization, hazard mitigation, and rebuilding across multiple mountain towns. The core loop is establishing and expanding settlements, satisfying population needs, building trade and transport networks, and solving environmental constraints like avalanches and weather. The text also directly states there is no combat or military aspect. This strongly supports city_builder as the primary archetype, with management_tycoon/colony_sim as adjacent secondary readings due to resource chains, needs management, and multi-settlement administration.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong and stays close to city-building and logistics management. Its best candidates capture Laysara’s economy chains, settlement building, and hazard/infrastructure problem-solving. The main gaps are that the set leans either toward roguelite/survival colony formats or much broader nation/empire simulators, while Laysara’s distinctive identity is a focused, combat-free mountain city builder with interconnected settlements and terrain-constrained transport engineering.
anchor_summary: Laysara: Summit Kingdom is a combat-free, single-player city builder centered on economy management, production chains, population needs, transport-network engineering, multi-settlement coordination, and environmental hazard mitigation in difficult mountain terrain. Best matches should share a systemic city-building/logistics core, optimization-heavy play, and survival-through-infrastructure rather than combat, grand strategy, or pure colony character simulation.
gap_notes: No candidate is an exact 'same' match for Laysara’s specific combination of multi-town mountain building, avalanche/weather response, and logistics-first route construction.

Several candidates are strong on city-building broadly but diverge in structure: Against the Storm is run-based roguelite; Anno 1800 adds diplomacy/warfare and empire play; Workers & Resources scales to nation simulation.
Banished is relevant as a combat-free survival builder but is notably simpler and less network/logistics-centric than the anchor.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on multi-settlement management, citizen needs, production chains, hostile weather, and no-combat city-building. The biggest difference is its roguelite run structure and heavier reset/meta-progression loop.

Strong fit on terrain-aware construction, infrastructure routing, environmental threat preparation, and optimization-heavy sandbox settlement building. It is more colony survival and water/terraforming-driven than Laysara’s inter-town trade network focus.

Excellent match for logistics depth, transport planning, production chains, and systemic city management without combat focus. It is broader, denser, and more nation-sim than Laysara’s mountain-settlement challenge format.

Shares citizen-need ladders, inter-settlement trade, and logistics optimization in a city-building framework. Less survival-focused and more empire management with diplomacy and warfare, making it a looser fit.

A solid combat-free settlement survival city builder with resource balancing and population management. It is more traditional and simpler, with less emphasis on complex transport engineering and interconnected settlements.

title: The New Zealand Story: Untold Adventure

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a colorful 2D platformer and remake of the 1988 platformer The NewZealand Story. The gameplay loop is described as jumping and running through handcrafted levels, uncovering hidden areas, using weapons, vehicles, and power-ups, and facing challenging enemies and bosses. This supports a level-based side-scrolling action-platformer identity with platform navigation as the core verb and combat as a supporting but meaningful layer. Although the computed near-miss/candidate system surfaced beat_em_up from side-scrolling + melee + bosses, the text strongly centers platforming, responsive movement, precision, hidden secrets, and arcade stage challenge rather than crowd-control brawling, making action_platformer the better fit.

overall_note: The rail mostly targets the anchor's true gameplay identity as a retro-inspired 2D action-platformer rather than the misfired beat-'em-up taxonomy. Best candidates match the anchor's arcade stage-clear platforming, while the weakest entry drifts toward interconnected exploration rather than level-based action-platforming.
anchor_summary: A classic-style 2D side-scrolling action-platformer remake built around level-based progression, precise jumping, enemy hazards, weapons/power-ups, hidden areas, vehicles, and boss encounters in an arcade challenge structure.
gap_notes: No candidate is the original The NewZealand Story or a direct base/legacy pairing, which would be an obvious editorial comp for this remake.
The strongest fits are classic arcade-style action-platformers/remakes; the set becomes looser once it moves into more exploratory or more modern set-piece-driven platformers.
Wonder Boy: The Dragon's Trap is notably more exploration-heavy and interconnected than the anchor's stage-based arcade progression.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close match: retro arcade-origin 2D action-platforming, linear stage progression, precise jumps, projectile combat, hazards, and boss encounters align strongly with the anchor.

candidate_reviews.candidate_title: Alex Kidd in Miracle World DX
candidate_reviews.requested_title: Alex Kidd in Miracle World DX
candidate_reviews.rationale: Classic revival with level-based platforming, enemy encounters, bosses, and old-school difficulty. Slightly less direct because its signature rock-paper-scissors boss structure is more distinctive than the anchor's combat loop.

Another remake of a classic side-scrolling platformer with stage-based play, hazards, enemy interactions, and exploration within levels. Strong fit, though pogo-centric movement/combat makes it a bit more specialized.
Shares 2D action-platforming fundamentals, secrets, combat, and level/world structure, but it is more modern, momentum-driven, and set-piece focused than the anchor's tighter arcade-retro profile.

It has clear action-platformer DNA, but its large interconnected world, transformation-based gating, and exploration-heavy progression push it toward metroidvania structure rather than the anchor's straightforward stage-based arcade loop.

title: RAIDEN FIGHTERS REMIX COLLECTION

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies this as a compilation of the Raiden Fighters arcade shooting games, emphasizing pixel graphics, high-tempo action, scoring systems, and online rankings. That strongly indicates a classic arcade shoot-'em-up/bullet-hell style collection. However, that gameplay identity does not map cleanly to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype in the provided list, and forcing it into an unrelated archetype would be inaccurate.

overall_note: This candidate set is tightly aligned with the anchor's gameplay identity as a retro arcade vertical shmup collection/remaster rail. The best fits are other Raiden modern reissues and multi-game arcade shooter compilations, with DoDonPachi as a strong genre benchmark but slightly less format-aligned.
anchor_summary: RAIDEN FIGHTERS REMIX COLLECTION is a classic arcade vertical shoot-'em-up compilation: short replayable stage runs, high difficulty, score-driven mastery, pixel-era presentation, online rankings, and collection/remix features around the Raiden Fighters trilogy.
gap_notes: No direct Raiden Fighters individual entries or prior trilogy packages are present, so the rail leans on franchise-near and genre-near comps rather than exact title-level pairings.

The set is strongest on classic vertical arcade shmups and collections; the only notable looseness is that DoDonPachi Resurrection is a single-entry bullet-hell release rather than a compilation/remix package.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Modern Raiden reissue with the same arcade vertical shmup core loop, short high-score runs, vertical screen support, multiple modes, and leaderboard focus; an obvious franchise-near benchmark.
Another enhanced Raiden arcade shooter with world rankings, vertical play, score-focused side modes, and the same essential pick-up-and-master structure.
Excellent format match as a retro arcade shooter collection built around multiple classic shmups, TATE support, high difficulty, and replay for mastery rather than campaign progression.
Another strong compilation comp: multiple arcade shooters, vertical orientation, short intense runs, and collection-style packaging closely match the anchor's use case.
Very relevant genre benchmark for vertical bullet-hell, challenge, and scoring, but it is a single Cave-style release rather than a retro Raiden-like collection/remaster package.

title: Afterplace

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text consistently describe Afterplace as a single-player open-world adventure focused on free exploration, hidden paths, secrets, dungeons, monster fighting, NPC conversations, and multiple endings driven by player choices. The strongest identity signals are open-world exploration, handcrafted discovery, quest/exploration progression, branching narrative outcomes, and active melee-oriented combat against monsters. RPG elements like weapon upgrades, armor purchases, and choice-driven endings support a secondary western_narrative_rpg read, but the dominant presentation in the evidence is open-world action-adventure rather than a deeper stat- or party-driven RPG. Perspective is not explicitly stated in the stored evidence, but the remaining evidence is strong enough to support the archetype.

overall_note: This rail has a strong grasp of Afterplace's top-down exploration-and-discovery identity, but it leans a bit too far toward harder, more structured combat-adventure neighbors. The best fits are games centered on curiosity, secrets, nonlinear roaming, and light action progression; the weaker fits skew more boss-driven, mechanically demanding, or progression-gated than Afterplace's freer wandering style.
anchor_summary: Afterplace is a single-player, top-down/open-world action-adventure with light RPG layering: freeform exploration, minimal handholding, hidden paths and dungeons, simple hack-and-slash combat, NPC dialogue, quest/exploration progression, equipment upgrades, and choice-shaped endings. Its identity leans more toward discovery-first wandering and authored but optional narrative than toward highly technical combat or tightly gated metroidvania structure.
gap_notes: The set captures top-down exploration well, but several candidates are significantly harsher or more combat-technical than Afterplace.
The rail underrepresents games with strong NPC interaction, loose narrative choice, and low-handholding open wandering as core identity traits.
No true base-game/direct-pairing case exists here, so the top candidates should be judged by experiential similarity rather than franchise/editorial expectation.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on top-down exploration, hidden routes, secrets, item-driven progression, and minimal guidance. More puzzle-centric and more punishing in combat than Afterplace, but the curiosity-led discovery loop is an excellent fit.

Strong match for surreal top-down wandering, odd NPCs, nonlinear discovery, dungeon exploration, and lighter combat emphasis. Less open-world in structure, but it aligns well with Afterplace's strange, exploratory feel.

Shares retro top-down roaming, branching paths, secrets, upgrades, and action combat. However, it is much harsher, faster, and less dialogue/choice-driven, making it more combat-forward than Afterplace.

Good overlap in top-down melee action, secrets, exploration, and light stat progression. Its structure is more directed, boss-centric, and challenge-focused, so it misses some of Afterplace's freer open wandering.

Understandable comp for top-down exploration, shortcuts, weapons, upgrades, and NPC stakes, but the timer pressure, build complexity, and more technical combat push it toward a different audience expectation than Afterplace.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on top-down exploration, hidden routes, secrets, item-driven progression, and minimal guidance. More puzzle-centric and more punishing in combat than Afterplace, but the curiosity-led discovery loop is an excellent fit.

Strong match for surreal top-down wandering, odd NPCs, nonlinear discovery, dungeon exploration, and lighter combat emphasis. Less open-world in structure, but it aligns well with Afterplace's strange, exploratory feel.

Shares retro top-down roaming, branching paths, secrets, upgrades, and action combat. However, it is much harsher, faster, and less dialogue/choice-driven, making it more combat-forward than Afterplace.

Good overlap in top-down melee action, secrets, exploration, and light stat progression. Its structure is more directed, boss-centric, and challenge-focused, so it misses some of Afterplace's freer open wandering.

Understandable comp for top-down exploration, shortcuts, weapons, upgrades, and NPC stakes, but the timer pressure, build complexity, and more technical combat push it toward a different audience expectation than Afterplace.

title: Dice A Million

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store text consistently identifies Dice A Million as a 'roguelike deckbuilder' built around randomly generated runs, combining 120+ dice for synergies, equipping passive rings, choosing among multiple hands/classes, and overcoming escalating debts and bosses. That strongly matches a run-based deck-construction game with buildcraft, metaprogression/unlocks, tactical optimization, and boss encounters. Although the theme is dice rather than literal cards, the core gameplay identity is clearly deckbuilder/card-battler adjacent, and card_battler is the closest supported archetype in the taxonomy.

overall_note: The set has several very strong dice-deckbuilding comps, especially Astrea and Die in the Dungeon, but it mixes in a couple of looser luck/synergy roguelites that are adjacent rather than truly same-identity matches.
anchor_summary: Dice A Million appears to be a single-player run-based roguelike deckbuilder centered on building a bag/pool of dice, stacking passive ring effects, chasing high-synergy builds, and overcoming escalating debts and bosses through tactical optimization and luck management. Its closest comps should therefore be dice-driven deckbuilding roguelikes with strong run structure, drafting/buildcraft, and turn-based encounter decision-making.
gap_notes: The rail would be stronger if it concentrated more tightly on dice-based roguelike deckbuilders rather than broader luck-driven scoring/build-synergy games.
Candidates that emphasize direct encounter-based tactical runs fit better than economy/slot-score optimization alone, even when the 'debt' framing overlaps.
No obvious wrong-title mismatch is present, but the lower-ranked adjacent picks are noticeably less aligned on combat structure and core loop.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Explicit dice-deck-building roguelike with run-based pool construction, tactical turns, synergy optimization, and risk-managed rolls; this is the clearest same-identity comp.
Another direct 'deckbuilding, but with dice' roguelite built on drafting dice, relic synergies, and turn-based encounters; extremely close on loop and progression structure.
Very close run-based tactical deckbuilding roguelike with explicit dice manipulation powering drafted spells; slightly more combat-spell framed but still a strong fit.
Strong overlap in dice-driven roguelike runs, class variety, equipment/build synergies, and tactical encounter play, though its equipment-centric structure is a bit less deckbuilder-pool focused.
Shares roguelite buildcraft, luck-pushing, debt pressure, and break-the-engine scoring, but the slot-machine economy loop is notably different from a tactical dice deckbuilder with boss encounters.

title: City Hunter

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence establishes only that this is a single-player action/adventure remake tied to the City Hunter license. The descriptions are almost entirely promotional and story-focused, with no concrete gameplay information about perspective, traversal, combat loop, mission structure, world topology, or progression. Because the gameplay identity is not described with enough specificity to distinguish among multiple action-adventure archetypes, the evidence is too thin for a reliable taxonomy mapping.

overall_note: Both candidates are plausible only as broad classic action neighbors, not high-confidence matches. Double Dragon Neon is the safer rail entry because its straightforward stage-based action revival format aligns with what City Hunter might be, while Code of Princess EX adds much heavier beat-'em-up/RPG systems and multiplayer emphasis that may overshoot the anchor's likely identity.
anchor_summary: City Hunter has extremely thin gameplay evidence: only that it is a single-player action/adventure remake of a 1990 licensed game. The available text is almost entirely story and nostalgia marketing, with no reliable detail on perspective, combat style, mission flow, traversal, progression, or challenge structure. Similarity judgments therefore must stay conservative and focus on broad classic action format overlap rather than assuming a specific subgenre.
gap_notes: Anchor gameplay identity is underdescribed, so no candidate can be treated as a highly confident same-gameplay match.
Neither candidate is a licensed detective/shooter benchmark; both are inferred from generic retro action revival overlap.
Code of Princess EX appears substantially more system-heavy and mode-rich than the anchor evidence supports.

candidate_reviews.rationale: A classic stage-based action revival with straightforward brawling structure; a reasonable broad comp if City Hunter is also a retro linear action game, though the anchor lacks enough evidence to confirm a beat-'em-up match.

Shares anime-styled action and mission-based single-player content, but its multi-plane beat-'em-up combat, large roster, RPG customization, and multiplayer focus make it a looser and more system-heavy comparison.

title: Supernova

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is materially contradictory. Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently present Supernova as a space-themed Suika-style matching puzzle focused on combining planets for high score, while the OpenCritic description instead describes a sci-fi MOBA/strategy game with upgradeable armies, commanders, and crafting from drops. These imply fundamentally different gameplay identities, and neither line of evidence is strong enough to cleanly map into a supported Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype with confidence. Because the bundle appears to conflate different titles or contains unreliable metadata, classification should be rejected.

overall_note: The proposed rail is directionally correct if the anchor is treated as a Suika-like puzzle game. It includes two very strong direct comps, then several broader falling-piece puzzle neighbors that share spatial planning and score pressure but differ meaningfully in core loop and competitive focus.
anchor_summary: Anchor metadata is contradictory, but the strongest consistent gameplay evidence from Steam/Metacritic points to a single-player Suika-style drop-and-merge puzzle: combine planets in a confined space, avoid overflow, and chase high scores. Similarity should therefore be judged against physics-driven merge/stack puzzle play rather than the stray MOBA description.
gap_notes: Candidate quality drops after the two direct Suika-like matches; the rest are puzzle neighbors rather than close identity matches.
Several candidates skew heavily toward versus multiplayer, while the anchor evidence points to a primarily single-player score-chasing loop.
Because the anchor metadata is conflicted, confidence is limited; however, none of the provided candidates support the stray MOBA interpretation.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct Suika-style comparison: same merge-identical-objects loop, confined playfield, overflow loss condition, and score-maximizing spatial optimization.
Another near-direct match built on dropping, stacking, and merging identical items into larger ones while preventing overflow for higher scores.
Shares falling-piece spatial planning, chain building, and board management, but its match/chain combat structure and versus emphasis are materially different from Suika-style physics merging.
Strong puzzle pedigree with falling-piece pressure and space optimization, but tetromino line-clearing is a different core loop and the package leans more heavily into mode variety and multiplayer.

Broadly adjacent through arcade puzzle decision-making and spatial efficiency, but it mixes two distinct competitive puzzle rulesets and centers versus play more than a simple single-player merge-score loop.

title: Ella Stars

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Ella Stars as an 'action-rhythm adventure' built around 30 original songs, with explicit rhythm gameplay, epic/intense boss battles, perfect combo finishers, adjustable difficulty, and single-player play. That strongly supports rhythm_game as the core identity, with timing-based play and boss-focused encounters as the main loop. Evidence for exact camera/topology is not explicit, so those fields are left empty rather than inferred.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's music-driven action identity reasonably well, especially rhythm-action hybrids with authored encounters and boss emphasis. Best fits are games where combat or movement is materially gated by beat timing; weaker fits either drift toward broader action-adventure or pure rhythm play without Ella Stars' combat-heavy boss loop.
anchor_summary: Ella Stars appears to be a single-player action-rhythm game built around timing-based inputs, original songs, and boss-centric encounters, with melee/action elements, authored progression, adjustable difficulty, and a colorful hand-drawn presentation.
gap_notes: The rail has good rhythm-action coverage but only one candidate strongly reflects Ella Stars' explicit boss-centric action loop without drifting far in scale or structure.
Some candidates over-index on music theme or presentation while undershooting the anchor's melee/combat emphasis.
A tighter rail would prioritize rhythm games with direct action combat and staged boss encounters over broader music-adventure or abstract rhythm titles.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on rhythm-synced action, authored progression, stylish combat, and major boss encounters; it is larger-scale 3D character action, so not quite the same gameplay identity.
Strong fit through beat-timed action, stage-based progression, repeated challenge play, and boss fights, though its core loop leans more platformer than melee boss-action adventure.
Good match on song-by-song rhythm encounters and challenge-driven single-player structure, but its battles are more pattern-dodging rhythm sequences than Ella Stars' action-melee boss combat.

Shares music-driven action-adventure structure and memorable boss battles, but combat is less strictly rhythm-timing dependent and plays more like broader action-adventure than a true rhythm-action game.
Clear rhythm-game overlap with hand-drawn art, original tracks, and accessible solo play, but it lacks the anchor's combat-forward, boss-centric action layer.

Clear rhythm-game overlap with hand-drawn art, original tracks, and accessible solo play, but it lacks the anchor's combat-forward, boss-centric action layer.

title: Welcome to Doll Town

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a single-player, third-person horror experience centered on exploration, mystery-solving, eerie puzzles, and escape rather than combat. Key phrases include 'Third-Person Exploration,' 'solve eerie puzzles,' 'uncover the truth,' 'slow-burn descent into horror,' and reality/dream dissolution, which strongly align with psychological horror. The cursed village, haunted dollhouses, environmental storytelling, and emphasis on dread and mystery support a narrative-driven psychological horror classification more strongly than action_horror or survival_horror, both of which lack concrete combat evidence here.

overall_note: The set leans well toward third-person narrative horror with exploration and puzzle-solving. Best fits are psychological or low-combat survival horror games built around investigation and dread. A few candidates skew more toward formal survival-horror threat management or explicit combat than the anchor appears to.
anchor_summary: Single-player third-person horror focused on exploration, eerie puzzles, mystery unraveling, and escape/survival pressure, with psychological framing and little clear evidence of combat-heavy play.
gap_notes: No candidate is an obvious must-keep base-game/expansion style pairing for this anchor.
The strongest matches emphasize psychological horror, exploration, and puzzles; several others add heavier survival-horror systems than the anchor suggests.
The rail could be stronger with more clearly low-combat third-person narrative horror comps, but among the provided options the top half is solid.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on third-person psychological horror, investigation, environmental storytelling, puzzle-solving, and limited direct combat emphasis.
Strong match on third-person town exploration, puzzles, oppressive atmosphere, and psychological mystery, though it has more overt combat and survival-horror structure.
Good fit for exploration, puzzle-solving, evasion, and tense horror progression, but its fixed-camera style and stronger survival-horror fail-state systems make it less like the anchor.

candidate_reviews.requested_title: Fatal Frame: Maiden of Black Water
candidate_reviews.rationale: Shares Japanese supernatural horror, cursed locations, mystery-driven exploration, and even doll imagery, but camera-based combat is a much more central gameplay pillar.
Understandable as third-person story-driven horror with evasion and investigation, but it is more stealth-survival and pursuer-driven than the anchor's likely exploration-puzzle identity.

title: SealChain: Call of Blood

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies SealChain as a single-player roguelike centered on item-linking, formations/seals, random events, character builds, talent trees, and combat against monsters. However, the available text does not establish a strong fit to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It is not clearly a card battler despite combinatorial item synergies, not clearly turn-based tactics despite strategy language, and not supportable as co_op_action_roguelite because the evidence indicates single-player only. The Metacritic 'Top-Down Shoot-'Em-Up' label is too weak and isolated to anchor a shooter archetype. Overall the gameplay identity is real but falls between the available archetypes, so classification should be rejected rather than forced.

overall_note: The proposed rail captures the anchor's roguelike run structure and build-synergy focus reasonably well, with the best candidates emphasizing spatial inventory optimization or explosive item-combo scaling. The biggest gap is that several options match only one side of SealChain's identity: either action horde combat without the formation puzzle layer, or strategic placement systems without comparable combat feel.
anchor_summary: SealChain: Call of Blood appears to be a single-player roguelike built around combinatorial item synergies: linking items, arranging formations to create seals, random events, character-specific builds, talent trees, and combat against monsters. Its identity seems to sit between action roguelite combat and a highly strategic inventory/formation optimization layer, with the spatial item system being the most distinguishing feature.
gap_notes: No candidate looks nearly identical; the rail lacks a true same-game benchmark for SealChain's specific item-linking and seal-formation combat identity.
The strongest fits are games with inventory-layout or placement-based build optimization, which seems more central to the anchor than generic roguelike overlap.
Pure action roguelike shooters capture the run-based combat and item scaling, but miss SealChain's distinctive formation/linking system.
Strategic placement-focused roguelites can fit conceptually, but become weaker when their combat structure diverges too far into deckbuilding, auto-battling, or indirect control.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Best overall fit: single-player action roguelike runs with monster survival plus inventory organization as a core progression puzzle. It lacks SealChain's exact linking/seal formation system, but the spatial optimization layer is a very close mechanical neighbor.

Very strong match on run-based item placement, adjacency-style synergies, and build definition through bag layout. Ranked below God of Weapons because its combat structure is more turn-based/deckbuilding-oriented and less evidently aligned with SealChain's action framing.

Strong benchmark for top-down roguelike runs, monster combat, randomization, and wild item-combo mutation. It is less precise because the core differentiation in SealChain seems to be spatial linking/formation optimization, which Isaac does not center.

Understandable pick for combinatorial placement strategy, roguelite structure, and class/build progression. Still a stretched fit because Loop Hero's indirect auto-combat and card/terrain placement loop differ substantially from SealChain's apparent direct combat and item-linking system.

Shares top-down roguelite runs, fast scaling, multiple characters, and heavy item stacking. But its wave-survival arena structure and comparatively simple equipment logic make it a looser fit than candidates with true spatial placement or formation mechanics.

title: Stillborn Slayer

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Stillborn Slayer as a single-player dark fantasy action roguelike with fast hack-and-slash combat, procedural runs, brutal difficulty, boss fights, dodging/rolling, and a grim horror-inflected tone. However, the evidence does not firmly establish a supported taxonomy archetype from the allowed list. The strongest identity is run-based action roguelite, but the available archetype list only includes co_op_action_roguelite, which contradicts the explicitly single-player framing. Alternative fits such as soulslike_action_rpg or character_action remain too weak because perspective is not established, progression is described as run/repetition-based rather than RPG progression, and the copy also emphasizes bullet-hell roguelike structure over a stable campaign RPG format. Because the title's core gameplay identity appears to fall between unsupported/insufficiently evidenced archetypes, classification should be rejected for now.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong because most candidates match the anchor's run-based action-roguelike identity, boss-focused combat, and high-difficulty loop. The main weakness is that a few picks lean more narrative/meta-progression heavy or more action-RPG/co-op adjacent than the anchor's more purely combat-driven single-player structure.
anchor_summary: Stillborn Slayer appears to be a single-player dark fantasy action roguelike/roguelite centered on fast, punishing hack-and-slash combat, dodge/roll skill, boss fights, procedural runs, replayability through repeated deaths, and a grim horror-inflected atmosphere. The strongest gameplay identity is run-based combat mastery rather than a campaign-driven soulslike RPG.
gap_notes: The anchor reads much more like a top-down or otherwise run-based action roguelike than a soulslike action RPG; best comps should prioritize procedural runs and death-loop mastery.
Dark fantasy tone is helpful, but the strongest matches are the games that also share fast melee combat, dodge timing, boss repetition, and procedural structure.
Children of Morta is the loosest fit here because its family progression, story emphasis, and co-op profile pull it away from the anchor's more isolated run-combat identity.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close fit on dark fantasy action-roguelite structure, fast melee combat, repeated runs, boss progression, and skill-based survival in a bleak world.
Excellent gameplay match: punishing run-based action, dodge timing, melee emphasis, traps, bosses, and procedural replayability with minimal story distraction.
A core benchmark for fast action-roguelike runs, enemy-pattern mastery, bosses, and build variation, though it is more narrative-driven and meta-progression heavy.
Shares action-roguelite runs, skilled dodge-centric combat, bosses, and bleak mood, but the waking-life/social layer makes its structure less purely combat-run focused.
Reasonable overlap in hack-and-slash roguelite combat and procedural dungeons, but its story-forward family progression, RPG framing, and co-op support make it a looser comp.

The strongest gameplay identity is run-based combat mastery rather than a campaign-driven soulslike RPG.

title: Wayblazer Dämmerung

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently frame the game as a 30+ hour JRPG with a party-based adventure, explorable isometric dungeons, a world map linking towns and quests, and explicit turn-based battles where the player chooses each party member's actions and plans turns tactically. The text also emphasizes non-linear progression after the prologue, several branching endings, relationship/trust systems with party members and towns, and equipment/resource loops for commissioning gear. That combination strongly supports jrpg_story_rpg as the core identity, with some tactical overlap from its turn-based party combat.

overall_note: The rail has a solid JRPG center and mostly respects the anchor’s party-based turn-based campaign structure. Its best comps match the classic story-driven JRPG loop, but it misses an even tighter emphasis on isometric exploration, branching/nonlinear progression, and town-development/resource-supply systems. One candidate leans more tactical RPG than core JRPG, which weakens the set slightly.
anchor_summary: Wayblazer Dämmerung is a single-player, party-based JRPG with overworld/town-to-isometric-dungeon exploration, explicit turn-based command combat, non-linear destination choice, branching story outcomes, relationship/trust progression, and a material-gathering plus town supply/crafting loop tied to party gearing and regional progression.
gap_notes: The set is strong on traditional turn-based JRPGs but lighter on games with comparable town trust, supply-chain, or crafting-for-progression loops.
Most candidates match story-driven party play, but fewer reflect the anchor’s isometric dungeon exploration and explicit tactical turn planning cadence.
Regalia is understandable because of kingdom restoration and bonding systems, but its tactical-grid identity is more offset from the anchor than the top JRPG comps.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close match on story-driven party JRPG structure, continent exploration, dungeon crawling, turn-based battles, and gear/crafting progression. Less aligned on town-trust and supply systems, but the core play loop is highly compatible.

Strong fit for overworld-to-dungeon JRPG flow, turn-based party combat, rebellion/war narrative framing, ally recruitment, and base-building progression. Broader roster focus makes it a bit less tightly matched than the top pick.

Good comparison for retro-style, story-driven party RPG combat and anti-regime political campaign structure. Feels more linear and less systems-rich around exploration, town development, and resource loops than the anchor.

Shares turn-based JRPG combat, party growth, town interaction, and open destination choice. The eight-protagonist chapter format and distinct path-action structure make its campaign identity looser than the anchor’s unified party adventure.

Overlap exists in party management, kingdom restoration, bonding, and turn-based play, but its primary identity is tactical RPG with positional/grid combat and stronger management sim framing. That is a meaningful step away from the anchor’s classic JRPG loop.

title: PancitoMerge

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence strongly indicates a casual single-player merge/falling-block style puzzle game focused on dropping, stacking, and merging breads for score, with light customer-request interactions and leaderboard play. However, the available taxonomy archetypes do not include a general merge puzzle, falling-block puzzle, or score-attack puzzle category. The computed candidate of hidden_object_puzzle is not supported by the described gameplay loop, which centers on spatial dropping/merging rather than hidden-object searching. Because the game's identity is clear but does not cleanly fit any allowed archetype, it should be rejected rather than force-mapped.

overall_note: The rail has two excellent gameplay matches at the top, a couple of reasonable arcade-puzzle neighbors, and one clear misfire caused by a title/data mismatch. Best fits are other drop-and-merge overflow-management score chasers, not just any abstract puzzle game.
anchor_summary: PancitoMerge is a casual single-player Suika-like puzzle game: you drop items into a container, use physics and positioning to merge matching pieces into larger ones, avoid topping out, and chase high scores in short replayable runs, with a light customer-order wrinkle.
gap_notes: The strongest identity signal is Suika-style falling/physics merge puzzle play; candidates matching that loop fit far better than traditional line-clear or versus puzzle games.
Tetris-family games share stack pressure and short-session score play, but their clear rules, piece control, and mode emphasis are materially different from merge-evolution physics puzzles.
One candidate is clearly wrong because the actual game data is for 8Bit Hero rather than 2048, making it a poor similar-games recommendation despite the requested title rationale.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Near-direct match: drop objects into a container, merge identical items into larger ones, manage physics and overflow, and play for score in short repeatable runs.
Very close Suika-like structure with item dropping, same-item merging, overflow fail state, and leaderboard/high-score focus; slightly less iconic than Suika Game as a benchmark.

Good arcade-puzzle neighbor through falling-piece spatial management, topping-out pressure, and replayable runs, but line clears and tetromino manipulation are a different core loop from physics-based merging.

Shares fast puzzle sessions and stack/chaining pressure, but its match-4/Tetris hybrid rules and strong competitive multiplayer framing make it a looser fit for a cozy single-player merge-drop puzzle.
Poor fit: the actual candidate is 8Bit Hero, a co-op hack-and-slash action game, not 2048. Its gameplay identity does not meaningfully overlap with PancitoMerge.
